title: Bank Statement Analysis for Trucking Owner-Operators
meta: Owner-operator truckers have irregular load payments and high fuel costs. Learn how lenders analyze trucking bank statements for business loans, equipment financing, and mortgage qualification.
content:
Owner-Operator Trucking Cash Flow

Independent truckers — owner-operators who own or lease their own equipment — are a significant segment of self-employed borrowers. Their bank statements reflect a business model with high gross revenue, high variable costs (fuel, maintenance, insurance), and highly irregular payment timing.

How Trucking Revenue Reaches a Bank Account

  • Factoring company deposits: Many truckers factor their invoices — selling them to a factoring company for immediate payment. Deposits appear under the factoring company's name (e.g., "RTS Financial," "OTR Capital," "Triumph Business Capital") at 90–97% of invoice value
  • Direct shipper/broker payments: Net-30 ACH deposits from freight brokers (Coyote, Echo, C.H. Robinson) or direct shippers
  • Quick Pay settlements: Same-day or next-day payments from load boards at a small discount

The Factoring Complication

When an owner-operator factors invoices, the deposit to their bank account is the factored amount — already reduced by the factoring fee (3–10% of invoice). This is the net amount they actually receive. Lenders should use this as the income figure — don't gross it up, as the factoring cost is a real business expense.

High Variable Costs

Trucking has significant variable costs that create large irregular outflows:

  • Fuel: Fuel card charges (often through Comdata, EFS, or WEX) appearing as daily or weekly debits
  • Maintenance and repairs: Large unpredictable outflows for breakdowns, tires, oil changes
  • Insurance: Monthly or quarterly truck insurance premiums
  • Truck payments: Equipment financing for the tractor/trailer

Lenders using StatementScrub can see net cash flow (deposits minus all withdrawals) alongside gross deposits — critical for understanding actual profitability after trucking costs.

Irregular Income Pattern

Owner-operator income is highly variable week to week — hot freight markets, slow seasons (January–February, post-holiday), breakdowns, and time off all affect monthly deposits. This is normal, not a red flag. Twelve months of statements is essential for accurate averaging.

What Lenders Look For

  • 12-month average monthly deposits from factoring or direct pay
  • Fuel card charges confirming active operation
  • Equipment loan payments showing investment in the business
  • NSF history — truckers managing cash flow carefully rarely overdraft
  • No excessive personal expenses through the business account

Loan Products for Owner-Operators

  • Commercial truck financing: For purchasing or refinancing tractors and trailers
  • Working capital loans / MCA: For covering expenses during slow freight periods
  • Mortgage qualification: Non-QM bank statement programs work well for truckers whose tax returns show low net income after deductions

Related: Bank statement analysis for trucking and transportation | Income verification without tax returns

Bottom Line

Owner-operator bank statements require understanding of the factoring model, high variable costs, and seasonal freight patterns. Lenders who analyze 12-month averages and understand trucking economics will find many owner-operators to be creditworthy borrowers who look complicated on paper but are actually reliable income earners.
